title: Early Hinduism and Early Buddhism
description: Practice your skills with a quiz on early Hinduism and Buddhism!

assessment_items.question_content:
| **Brahmin** | Kshatriya | Vaishya 
- | - | - | - 
**Age for initiation** | 8 | 11 | 12
**Last age for initiation** | 16 | 22 | 24
**Seasons for initiation** | spring | hot season | autumn
**Upper garment** | black antelope | spotted deer | goat or cow
**Lower garment** | linen or hempen | cotton or linen | woolen
**Size of staff** | reaches top of head | reaches forehead | reaches nose

Chart adapted from Brian K. Smith, "Ritual, Knowledge, and Being: Initiation and Veda Study in Ancient India", 1986.

**A historian researching the Vedic caste system and religion would most likely find this chart useful as evidence of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The importance of colors in iconography
The importance of military staffing strength
The importance of social stratification
The political importance of seasons

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>“All Buddhists have had one and the same aim, which is the 'extinction of self,' the dying out of separate individuality, and their teachings and practices have generally tended to foster such easily recognizable spiritual virtues as serenity, detachment, consideration and tenderness for others. In the Scriptures, the Dharma has been compared to a taste. The word of the Buddha is there defined as that which has the taste of Peace, the taste of Emancipation, the taste of Nirvana.”

> From Edward Conze, _A Short History of Buddhism_ (2007).

**The ideas about the extinction of the self expressed in the excerpt are most similar to which of the following ideas?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: Hindu concept of mokhsha
Vedic concept of the varnas
Confucian concept of filial piety
Jewish concept of monotheism

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]


**The data presented in the map best support which of the following comparative statements about the spread of Buddhism and Hinduism?**

[[☃ radio 3]]

options.alt: Map showing the spread of Buddhism through Asia.
options.caption: Map showing the spread of Buddhism through Asia. Image credit: [Flickr](https://www.flickr.com/photos/zensquared/6112648107)

choices.content: Buddhism extended further beyond the Indian subcontinent than Hinduism did as a result of trade and missionary activity
Buddhism extended further beyond the Indian subcontinent than Hinduism did as a result of military conquest
Hinduism extended further beyond the Indian subcontinent than Buddhism did as a result of state promotion of the religion
Hinduism extended further beyond the Indian subcontinent than Buddhism did as a result of its strong monastic tradition

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>Not by matted hair, not by lineage, not by caste, does one become a Brahmin. He is a Brahmin in whom there are truth and righteousness. He is blessed.

From: _The Dhammapada_

**The ideas expressed in the excerpt most directly challenged which of the following prevailing ideologies in Ancient India? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Vedic ideals regarding social hierarchy
Buddhist ideals regarding religious sincerity
Confucian ideals regarding moral uprightness
Daoist ideals regarding political order

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>People cleave to their worldly possessions and selfish passions so blindly as to sacrifice their own lives for them. They are like a child who tries to eat a little honey smeared on the edge of a knife. The amount is by no means sufficient to appease his appetite, but he runs the risk of wounding his tongue.

From: _The Sutra of Forty-Two Chapters _, c. 67 CE

**The ideas about  worldly possessions expressed in the excerpt are most consistent with which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Confucian ethics
Buddhist beliefs
Filial piety
Hindu social structures

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>Both adopt one and the same discipline (Vinaya), and they have in common the prohibitions of the five skandhas (‘groups of offences’), and also the practice of the Four Noble Truths. Those who worship the Bodhisattvas and read the Mahayana Sutras are called the Mahayanists, while those who do not perform these are called the Hinayanists.

From the writings of Yijing (I-tsing), late seventh century CE

**The excerpt provided is best understood in the context of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: The spread of Buddhism to China during the Han dynasty
The development of different branches of Buddhism
The codification of the Four Noble Truths in Buddhist scripture
The development of syncretic belief systems in India

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>Beloved-of-the-Gods, King Piyadasi, speaks thus: There is no gift like the gift of the Dhamma (Dharma), no acquaintance like acquaintance with Dhamma, no distribution like distribution of Dhamma, and no kinship like kinship through Dhamma. And it consists of this: proper behavior towards servants and employees, respect for mother and father, generosity to friends, companions, relations, Brahmans and ascetics, and not killing living beings. Therefore a father, a son, a brother, a master, a friend, a companion or a neighbor should say: "This is good, this should be done." One benefits in this world and gains great merit in the next by giving the gift of the Dhamma.

From: _The Edicts of Ashoka_, c. 269 BCE to 232 BCE

**Which of the following best explains Emperor Ashoka’s characterization of the Dhamma as a “gift”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Ashoka supported the spread of Jainism by offering rewards for conversion
Ashoka supported the spread of Hinduism by distributing religious scriptures
Ashoka supported the spread of Buddhism by praising its teachings
Ashoka supported the spread of Din-illahi by proclaiming a religious prophecy

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>“All Buddhists have had one and the same aim, which is the 'extinction of self', the dying out of separate individuality, and their teachings and practices have generally tended to foster such easily recognizable spiritual virtues as serenity, detachment, consideration and tenderness for others. In the Scriptures, the Dharma has been compared to a taste. The word of the Buddha is there defined as that which has the taste of Peace, the taste of Emancipation, the taste of Nirvana.”

From Edward Conze, _A Short History of Buddhism_ (2007).

**The ideas about the separate self expressed in the excerpt best represent a continuity with which of the following religious traditions?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: Hinduism
Confucianism
Daoism
Sikhism

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>“Early Buddhist art did not portray the Buddha, but drawings made in the seventh and eighth centuries depict different scenes in the historical Buddha’s life, as well as other buddhas and bodhisattvas, who were believed to have turned back at the moment of attaining nirvana to help their fellow Buddhists still on earth.”

From Valerie Hansen, _The Silk Road: A New History_ (2015).

**Which of the following most directly contributed to the developments described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: The emergence of Mahayana Buddhism
The spread of Theravada Buddhism
The development of Greco-Buddhism
The codification of the first Buddhist texts

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**Taken together, these images best represent what historical continuity within Hinduism?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Image of the Hindu deity Vishnu from a temple in Central India, from the sixth century CE
options.caption: Image of the Hindu deity Vishnu from a temple in Central India, from the sixth century CE. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-images/f746ac1edb0b85c7ca2f564b3f651667216e13f5.jpg)

options.alt: Hindu statue of kneeling woman, from eleventh century Cambodia.
options.caption: Hindu statue of kneeling woman, from eleventh century Cambodia. Image credit: [Wikimedia](http://www.metmuseum.org/toah/works-of-art/1972.147/)

choices.content: The ongoing use of art to demonstrate religious devotion in Hinduism
The use of similar forms of dress in Hindu religious rituals across regions
The syncretism of different religious beliefs as Hinduism spread
The spread of similar materials to construct Hindu statues as a result of long-distance trade

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>Four times, five, I ran amok from my dwelling,
	having gained no peace of awareness,
	my thoughts out of control.
So I went to a trustworthy nun.
She taught me the Dhamma:
	aggregates, sense spheres, & elements.
Hearing the Dhamma,
	I did as she said.
For seven days I sat in one spot,
absorbed in rapture and bliss.
On the eighth, I stretched out my legs,
	having burst the mass
	of darkness.

From _The Therigatha_, c. 600 BCE

**The excerpt provided is best understood in the context of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 3]]

choices.content: The emergence of a Buddhist monastic tradition
The codification of early Hindu scriptures
The development of Sikh institutions
The spread of Buddhist beliefs

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Edicts like that shown in the image contributed most directly to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Stone with letters carved into it.
options.caption: Edict in Rock Inscription in Greek and Aramaic,  3rd century BC by King Ashoka, Kandahar. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kandahar_Bilingual_Rock_Inscription#/media/File:AsokaKandahar.jpg).

choices.content: State promotion of Hindu beliefs in Greece
Spread of Hellenic culture to India
Adoption of Greco-Roman beliefs in India
Development of Greco-Buddhism in Bactria
